q0325.reference:
There are 24 issues in the Software Bug Triage (BUGS) project associated with the API component. These issues primarily focus on standardizing REST endpoints, improving documentation via Swagger/OpenAPI, and addressing data consistency. A significant portion of the work involves datetime and timezone handling, such as fixing inconsistent timezone formats in JSON (BUGS-97), implementing ISO 8601 UTC serializers (BUGS-99), and normalizing input datetime fields (BUGS-100). Other key tasks include auditing API responses for sensitive data (BUGS-98), designing REST API v2 naming conventions (BUGS-77), and adding GraphQL endpoints as alternatives to REST (BUGS-89).

The complete list of relevant issue keys includes BUGS-98, BUGS-97, BUGS-93, BUGS-89, BUGS-85, BUGS-81, BUGS-77, BUGS-72, BUGS-68, BUGS-64, BUGS-60, BUGS-56, BUGS-52, BUGS-47, BUGS-43, BUGS-39, BUGS-35, BUGS-31, BUGS-27, BUGS-22, BUGS-18, BUGS-14, BUGS-10, and BUGS-6. q0228.reference:
In the last 30 days, the OPS project recorded 100 issues focused on infrastructure resilience, database migrations, and observability. These issues are categorized into three primary priority levels.

Highest priority issues (6 issues) center on immediate service availability, security, and critical SRE escalations. Key concerns include urgent Kubernetes 1.29 upgrades, PostgreSQL 12 end-of-life migrations, and resolving critical resource exhaustion in production databases. Notable issues include OPS-89, OPS-77, OPS-56, OPS-27, OPS-14, and OPS-2, which address emergency responses to API gateway latency, DNS resolution failures, and failing disaster recovery procedures.

High priority issues (12 issues) focus on incident prevention, system visibility, and scaling. Main operational themes involve database governance, such as the MongoDB to DocumentDB migration (OPS-85) and PgBouncer connection pooling (OPS-81). Other concerns include CI/CD performance, specifically GitLab runner optimization (OPS-64) and build cache improvements (OPS-60), alongside observability enhancements like SLO dashboards (OPS-35) and Elasticsearch tiering (OPS-47).

Medium priority issues (82 issues) represent the bulk of the project's work, focusing on incremental infrastructure hygiene and long-term SRE goals. Primary concerns include implementing distributed tracing with OpenTelemetry, standardizing logging formats (OPS-31), and addressing general CI/CD pipeline flakiness. These tasks aim to improve developer experience and provide better pipeline analytics (OPS-52) without the urgency of active incidents. Other relevant issues in this period include OPS-88, OPS-87, OPS-83, OPS-79, OPS-26, OPS-1, OPS-22, OPS-97, OPS-18, OPS-10, OPS-76, OPS-51, OPS-72, OPS-93, OPS-68, and OPS-70.

q0235.reference:
There are 46 issues identifying mobile app problems across the iOS and Android platforms. Android-specific issues frequently involve authentication and deep linking, including MFA enrollment crashes on Android 12+ BUGS-18, biometric prompt failures BUGS-20, and broken deep links for password resets BUGS-64. Android also faces UI bugs like the keyboard obscuring text fields SMP-15 and crashes when using iOS-style components SMP-80. Network handling is a concern on Android, where the app fails to reconnect after switching from Wi-Fi to 5G BUGS-56.

iOS-specific problems are characterized by high crash rates and rendering bugs. The app crashes when viewing attachments over 15MB due to memory overload BUGS-52, and users report issues with client storage clearing unexpectedly SMP-103. Rendering problems include gradients failing on iOS 17 SMP-34 and Metal backend issues SMP-114.

Cross-platform issues affect both operating systems, notably severe battery drain caused by excessive background polling BUGS-60 and high CPU usage during idle states BUGS-61. Performance jank is a major theme, with significant scroll performance degradation on long lists BUGS-72 and BUGS-75. Other shared problems include push notification delivery failures BUGS-15, BUGS-17, session synchronization delays BUGS-62, and general performance regressions BUGS-51. Project-specific mobile tasks also appear in CRM-58, CRM-77, CRM-79, CRM-92, and PLAT-2, covering mobile-responsive layouts and API integrations. Overall, Android struggles more with system-level integrations like MFA and networking, while iOS faces more memory-related crashes and rendering inconsistencies.

q0119.reference:
Analysis of CRM issue descriptions reveals several critical troubleshooting automation gaps that contribute to manual overhead and delayed resolution times. A primary gap is the lack of automated error message matching and resolution (CRM-85). Currently, agents must manually look up error codes because the existing knowledge base is not indexed by error patterns (CRM-86). This is compounded by the absence of OCR capabilities to extract error text from user-submitted screenshots (CRM-87), forcing agents to transcribe data manually.

Furthermore, there is a significant gap in automated technical context collection. Agents frequently engage in repetitive back-and-forth communication to gather basic environment details like OS and app versions (CRM-89). To address these inefficiencies, the system requires automated troubleshooting step suggestions that trigger when a ticket matches a known error pattern (CRM-88). Implementing these features would allow for auto-lookup of known issues and the delivery of step-by-step guides to users without manual agent intervention. Other identified gaps include the need for automated evidence gathering for specific ticket types like payment disputes (CRM-1, CRM-2) and the lack of proactive monitoring to identify patterns before they are reported by users. Addressing these gaps through OCR, fuzzy matching, and automated data compilation is essential for reducing the current manual burden on support teams.

q0107.reference: Analysis of high-priority issues in the BUGS project reveals four primary technical debt patterns. First, inconsistent API design and data handling are prevalent, characterized by mixed naming conventions and pagination patterns in the v1 REST API BUGS-77, as well as inconsistent timezone handling across endpoints BUGS-97. Second, there are significant gaps in network error handling and session management, particularly on Android where silent failures occur without retry logic BUGS-56, and session invalidations fail to propagate to mobile clients BUGS-14. Third, mobile performance issues stem from resource management debt, including scroll jank caused by main-thread rendering BUGS-72, excessive battery drain from undisciplined background syncing BUGS-60, and memory leaks in dashboard subscriptions BUGS-6. Finally, rate limiter bugs have emerged due to architectural oversights, such as the system incorrectly counting OPTIONS preflight requests against user quotas BUGS-85. Other notable debt includes missing Android 12+ biometric API handling BUGS-18, memory-intensive attachment loading on iOS BUGS-52, and a heavy reliance on polling rather than event-driven architecture BUGS-81. These patterns suggest a need for centralized middleware and standardized design patterns to address recurring stability and performance bottlenecks. Additional issues contributing to this landscape include BUGS-2, BUGS-64, BUGS-10, BUGS-43, BUGS-35, and BUGS-27.

q0212.reference:
Performance issues are documented across several projects, primarily focusing on mobile application responsiveness and infrastructure optimization. BUGS-26 also tracks degraded search latency.
